era_context: The Late Bronze Age is in severe crisis. This year falls within the period of the Late Bronze Age collapse, a multi-decade process of state failure, migration, and economic disruption. Major empires are fragmenting; the Sea Peoples are actively migrating and raiding; and long-distance trade networks are breaking down. Egypt under Ramesses III is mobilising to defend against external threats. The Hittite Empire is in terminal decline, and Mycenaean Greece is fragmenting.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe (no written records; archaeological evidence sparse)
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited synchronous documentation)
East Asia (contemporary Chinese records exist but are rarely cross-referenced with Mediterranean events)
The Americas (no contact with Old World)

events.title: Sea Peoples migrations and raids continue across the Eastern Mediterranean
events.description: The Sea Peoples continue their migrations and raids across the Eastern Mediterranean. Multiple groups are moving by sea and land, attacking coastal settlements and attempting to establish footholds in the Levant and Egypt. The Egyptian military is mobilised to defend against these incursions. The pressure on Egypt's borders is mounting. These incursions are part of a broader phenomenon of population displacement and migration driven by climate stress, famine, and the collapse of Bronze Age states.

sources.name: Medinet Habu temple reliefs and inscriptions

events.certainty_note: The Sea Peoples incursions are well-attested in Egyptian sources, but the specific dating of individual raids to -1188 versus -1187 or -1186 is uncertain. The assignment to -1188 is conventional.
cross_references: Part of the broader Sea Peoples phenomenon

events.title: Hittite Empire fragmenting; regional collapse accelerating
events.description: The Hittite Empire is fragmenting rapidly. The central authority is weakening, and regional governors are losing contact with the capital Hattusa. Invasion by the Kaska peoples from the north and pressure from the Sea Peoples are accelerating the collapse. The empire's administrative system is breaking down, and the state is approaching its final dissolution. Within a few years, the Hittite state will cease to exist as a major power.
key_figures: Suppiluliuma II (last Great King of the Hittites)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Hattusa and other Hittite sites

events.certainty_note: The fall of the Hittite Empire is archaeologically attested but not precisely dated. Scholarly estimates range from -1200 to -1150 BCE. The assignment to -1188 is approximate.
cross_references: Part of the Late Bronze Age collapse

events.title: Mycenaean Greece in advanced collapse; palace destruction and abandonment
events.description: The major palatial centres of Mycenaean Greece are destroyed or abandoned. The destruction is attributed to a combination of internal conflict, invasion, and the broader destabilisation of the Eastern Mediterranean. The loss of centralised administration and long-distance trade marks the transition from the Bronze Age to the Early Iron Age. Population displacement and migration are occurring, with some Greeks moving to Cyprus and the Levantine coast.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Mycenaean palace sites

events.certainty_note: The destruction of Mycenaean palaces is archaeologically confirmed but not precisely dated to individual years. Scholarly estimates range from -1200 to -1150 BCE.
cross_references: Part of the Late Bronze Age collapse; contemporary with the fall of the Hittite Empire

events.title: Levantine cities experiencing destruction and abandonment
events.description: The Levantine cities are experiencing destruction and abandonment. The collapse of the Hittite Empire removes a major political power; the fragmentation of Mycenaean Greece disrupts Aegean trade; and the Sea Peoples are raiding coastal settlements. Archaeological evidence shows burning, destruction, and the abandonment of settlements. Population displacement and migration are occurring as people flee the chaos.
events.certainty_note: The destruction of Levantine cities is archaeologically attested but not precisely dated to individual years. The assignment to -1188 is conventional.
cross_references: Part of the Late Bronze Age collapse; contemporary with the fall of the Hittite Empire and the fragmentation of Mycenaean Greece

disconfirming_evidence: The precise dating of events in -1188 is highly uncertain. The archaeological record does not provide year-by-year resolution; instead, it shows destruction layers and abandonment phases that span decades. The assignment of specific events to -1188 is largely conventional and reflects scholarly consensus chronologies that themselves have ±10–20 year uncertainties. The 'Sea Peoples' are often portrayed as a unified threat, but modern scholarship views them as a complex phenomenon. The role of climate change (drought, famine) in the collapse is increasingly emphasised, but the relative weight of climate versus military/political factors remains debated. Some scholars argue that the 'collapse' narrative overstates the uniformity and speed of change.
historiographic_note: The historical record for -1188 is extremely sparse. No specific events are dated to this year in contemporary sources; instead, scholars assign events to this year based on archaeological stratigraphy and chronological calculations. The Egyptian sources are the most detailed, but they are propagandistic and do not provide year-by-year narrative. The Hittite and Mycenaean records are fragmentary. The perspective of the Sea Peoples themselves is almost entirely lost. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, Central Asia, and East Asia are absent from the surviving record. The chronological synchronisation between Egyptian, Hittite, and Aegean chronologies remains uncertain by ±10–20 years.

graph_edges.note: Sea Peoples incursions are ongoing across multiple years
The collapse of the Hittite Empire is contemporary with Sea Peoples incursions
The collapse of Mycenaean Greece is contemporary with the fall of the Hittite Empire
The fall of the Hittite Empire accelerates the destruction of Levantine cities
